content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 4, 1991

Patricia Newman
City Clerk
City of Dunsmuir
Dunsmuir, CA 96025

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-515

Dear Ms. Newman:

This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the duties and responsibilities of City of Dunsmuir
Councilmember Julie Signor under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
Councilmember Signor to disqualify herself from participating in
decisions regarding litigation against Southern Pacific?

CONCLUSION

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require
Councilmember Signor to disqualify herself from participating in
decisions regarding litigation against Southern Pacific because it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material
financial effect on Southern Pacific which is a source of income
to the councilmember.

FACTS

The City of Dunsmuir is considering litigation against
Southern Pacific in relation to the toxic chemical spill into the
Sacramento River which occurred on July 14, 1991.

_________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-515
Page 2


Councilmember Signor's spouse is an employee of Southern
Pacific. On behalf of Councilmember Signor, you seek our advice
to determine whether her husband's employment gives rise to a
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act with regards to
decisions affecting Southern Pacific.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest. Councilmember Signor is a public official.
(Section 82048.)

Economic Interests

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a governmental decision, within the meaning of Section
87100, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally, on the official, or on a member of the
official's immediate family² or on:

Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

Section 87103(c).

You have advised us that Councilmember Signor's spouse is an
employee of Southern Pacific. Section 82030 provides that the
income of an individual also includes any community property
interest in the income of a spouse. Consequently, if Southern
Pacific has been a source of income to the councilmember's spouse
of $500 or more within 12 months of any decision regarding
Southern Pacific, the councilmember's disqualification is required
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on Southern Pacific. (Shaw Advice
Letter, No. I-91-108.)


2
An official's "immediate family" are his or her spouse and
dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Our File No. A-91-515
Page 3


Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a
Governmental Decision

     A public official makes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency.  (Regulation 18700(b).)  Additionally, a public
official participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

          (1)  Negotiates, without significant
     substantive review, with a governmental entity or
     private person regarding the decision; or

          (2)  Advises or makes recommendations to the
     decision-maker, either directly or without
     significant intervening substantive review, by:

               (A)  Conducting research or making any
          investigation which requires the exercise of
          judgment on the part of the official or designated
          employee and the purpose of which is to influence
          the decision; or

               (B)  Preparing or presenting any report,
          analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which
          requires the exercise of judgment on the part of
          the official or designated employee and the purpose
          of which is to influence the decision.

                    Regulation 18700(c).

     With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, any other attempt to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency.  Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer.  (Regulation 18700.1.)

     Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that Southern
Pacific, which is a source of income to Councilmember Signor, will
be materially affected by governmental decisions regarding
potential litigation, Councilmember Signor must not only
disqualify herself from participating in formal decisions of the
city council which may affect Southern Pacific, but she must also
abstain from attempting to influence the decisions by
communicating with other members of the city council or with staff
regarding the decisions.

Our File No. A-91-515
Page 4

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-
991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198. The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

It is reasonably foreseeable that a decision to initiate
litigation against Southern Pacific will have a financial effect
on Southern Pacific which is a source of income to the
councilmember. For example, a decision to initiate litigation
will result in expenditures incurred by Southern Pacific defending
the allegations. Conversely, a decision not to initiate
litigation will result in savings for Southern Pacific. Thus, it
is foreseeable that decisions regarding the initiation of
litigation against Southern Pacific will have a foreseeable
financial effect on Southern Pacific. If this effect will be
material, as discussed below, Councilmember Signor must disqualify
himself from participating in these decisions unless the "public
generally" exception applies.

Materiality

Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining
whether an official's economic interest in a decision is
materially affected by a decision. If the official's economic
interest is directly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1
provides the appropriate standard for assessing materiality.
Regulation 18702.1(b) states that a person or business entity is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official's agency.

(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.

Decisions of the city council regarding litigation against
Southern Pacific will have a direct effect on Southern Pacific.
This is so because, for purposes of Regulation 18702.1(b)(2),

Our File No. A-91-515
Page 5

Southern Pacific will be named in the litigation and will be the
subject of the proceedings.

When a business entity which has been a source of income to a
public official of $250 or more in the 12 months preceding the
decision will be directly affected by a governmental decision, the
effect of a governmental decision is material. Accordingly, the
councilmember must disqualify herself from participating in any
governmental decision regarding Southern Pacific.

Public Generally

Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all members of
the public in the same manner, disqualification may be required
unless the effect of the decision on the source of income is the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public.
(Regulation 18703.) The "public generally" comprises all persons
within the jurisdiction of the public official. (In re Owen
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 81.) Therefore, the "public generally" as
applicable to the jurisdiction of a member of the Dunsmuir City
Council are all persons within the City of Dunsmuir.

Any decision to initiate litigation against Southern Pacific
will have a financial effect on Southern Pacific. However, this
decision will not affect other residents of the city in a similar
manner. Accordingly, the "public generally" exception is
inapplicable to your facts and the councilmember's
disqualification is required.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.³

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Blanca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:bb

___________________________
³ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in making, or otherwise using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a financial interest. Councilmember Signor is a public official. (Section 82048.)

Economic Interests

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial interest in a governmental decision, within the meaning of Section 87100, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a member of the official’s immediate family2 or on:

Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

Section 87103(c).

You have advised us that Councilmember Signor’s spouse is an employee of Southern Pacific. Section 82030 provides that the income of an individual also includes any community property interest in the income of a spouse. Consequently, if Southern Pacific has been a source of income to the councilmember’s spouse of $500 or more within 12 months of any decision regarding Southern Pacific, the councilmember’s disqualification is required if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on Southern Pacific. (Shaw Advice Letter, No. I-91-108.)

2 An official’s "immediate family" are his or her spouse and dependent children. (Section 82029.)

Making, Participating in Making, or Attempting to Influence a Governmental Decision

A public official makes a governmental decision or participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the public official votes on a matter, commits the agency to a course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of the agency. (Regulation 18700(b).) Additionally, a public official participates in a governmental decision when, acting within the authority of his or her position, the public official:

(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental entity or private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker, either directly or without significant intervening substantive review, by:

(A) Conducting research or making any investigation which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision; or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official or designated employee and the purpose of which is to influence the decision.

Regulation 18700(c).

With regard to a governmental decision which is within or before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency, an official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer. (Regulation 18700.1.)

Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that Southern Pacific, which is a source of income to Councilmember Signor, will be materially affected by governmental decisions regarding potential litigation, Councilmember Signor must not only disqualify herself from participating in formal decisions of the city council which may affect Southern Pacific, but she must also abstain from attempting to influence the decisions by communicating with other members of the city council or with staff regarding the decisions.

Foreseeability

The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere possibility; however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Comm. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198. The Act seeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at 823.)

It is reasonably foreseeable that a decision to initiate litigation against Southern Pacific will have a financial effect on Southern Pacific which is a source of income to the councilmember. For example, a decision to initiate litigation will result in expenditures incurred by Southern Pacific defending the allegations. Conversely, a decision not to initiate litigation will result in savings for Southern Pacific. Thus, it is foreseeable that decisions regarding the initiation of litigation against Southern Pacific will have a foreseeable financial effect on Southern Pacific. If this effect will be material, as discussed below, Councilmember Signor must disqualify herself from participating in these decisions unless the "public generally" exception applies.

Materiality

Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining whether an official's economic interest in a decision is materially affected by a decision. If the official's economic interest is directly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.1 provides the appropriate standard for assessing materiality. Regulation 18702.1(b) states that a person or business entity is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.

(3) A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity.

Decisions of the city council regarding litigation against Southern Pacific will have a direct effect on Southern Pacific. This is so because, for purposes of Regulation 18702.1(b)(2),

Southern Pacific will be named in the litigation and will be the subject of the proceedings.

When a business entity which has been a source of income to a public official of $250 or more in the 12 months preceding the decision will be directly affected by a governmental decision, the effect of a governmental decision is material. Accordingly, the councilmember must disqualify herself from participating in any governmental decision regarding Southern Pacific.

Public Generally

Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision is material, disqualification is required only if the effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section 87100.) If the decision does not affect all members of the public in the same manner, disqualification may be required unless the effect of the decision on the source of income is the same as the effect on a significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18703.) The "public generally" comprises all persons within the jurisdiction of the public official. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 81.) Therefore, the "public generally" as applicable to the jurisdiction of a member of the Dunsmuir City Council are all persons within the City of Dunsmuir.

Any decision to initiate litigation against Southern Pacific will have a financial effect on Southern Pacific. However, this decision will not affect other residents of the city in a similar manner. Accordingly, the "public generally" exception is inapplicable to your facts and the councilmember's disqualification is required.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry. Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.3

QUESTION: Do the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require Councilmember Signor to disqualify herself from participating in decisions regarding litigation against Southern Pacific?

CONCLUSION: The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act require Councilmember Signor to disqualify herself from participating in decisions regarding litigation against Southern Pacific because it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have a material financial effect on Southern Pacific which is a source of income to the councilmember.
